risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "Treatment allocation was done by random permutation within blocks with block sizes of 4 and 6 using a computerized algorithm." Comment: This describes a proper random sequence generation method (computerized random permutation within blocks), which is sufficient to judge the allocation sequence as truly random.

Quote: "Treatment allocation was done by random permutation within blocks with block sizes of 4 and 6 using a computerized algorithm." Comment: This describes the random sequence generation, but it does not state how the upcoming assignment was concealed from the person enrolling participants. There is no explicit mention of central allocation, sealed opaque envelopes, identical containers, or any other mechanism preventing foreknowledge, so allocation concealment cannot be judged as low risk.

risk_of_bias.domain: Blinding of participants and personnel (performance bias)
risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "Participants were randomly assigned to 1 of the 2 conditions: (1) MAP access or (2) usual care" and "The control group did not have access to MAP." Comment: This was an open-label web-portal intervention with no indication that participants or treating personnel were blinded; the intervention could readily be recognized by participants and the nurse case-manager, and the outcomes included patient-reported quality of life and asthma control, which could plausibly be influenced by awareness of allocation.

Summary: Outcome assessment was by multiple sources, including patient self-report for MAQLQ/ACT/PHQ/EQ-VAS/BMQ/self-efficacy, but the key primary control outcome was based on automated/administrative data: "Asthma control at 6 months was evaluated by examining potential overuse (yes/no) of rescue fast-acting bronchodilators ... based on units dispensed for prescriptions as recorded in the provincial drug database" and "Asthma-related ED visits or hospitalizations were extracted from the Quebec provincial insurance database using validated algorithms." Comment: For the primary outcome used to assess asthma control, measurement came from objective administrative records/validated algorithms and would not be influenced by lack of blinding, even though participant-reported outcomes were unblinded.

Quote: "Of the 100 individuals who were randomized, 16 were lost to follow-up at 3 months in the intervention group... and ... Three individuals in the control group were lost to follow-up at 3 months postrandomization." Also: "Analyses were conducted using an intention-to-treat analysis." Comment: Attrition was moderate and somewhat higher in the intervention arm, but overall loss to follow-up was still about 19% (19/100) and the paper gives reasons for most intervention dropouts, which were mainly logistical/time-related rather than clearly outcome-related. The study also used ITT analysis and sensitivity analyses with imputed missing data, which makes bias from missing data unlikely.
